title: Traffic demand modeling using agent model
summary: Building a digital twin of road traffic with agent-based simulation (2022~2025): macroscopic transport simulation is integrated with activity-based modeling to reproduce realistic traffic flows and predict traffic volumes at the micro-analysis-zone level.

paragraphs: Development of a digital twin for road traffic using autonomous driving Level 4/4+ big data (2022~2025). A multi-scale traffic simulation space identical to the real world enables the analysis and prediction of situations that are infeasible to test in reality, such as future mixed traffic environments.

The macroscopic stage generates realistic traffic flows by producing origin-destination data at the MAZ (Micro Analysis Zone) level and predicting traffic volumes for each route. The generated data feeds the microscopic stage, where an activity-based model (ABM) dynamically reproduces individual travel behavior at the coordinate level.

sections.heading: Integrated simulation platform
bullets: Simulation manager data system and custom configuration schemes that generate result data tailored to the user's analytical purpose
Vehicle generation methodologies defining generation intervals, lane assignment priorities, and initial speeds to reproduce realistic traffic flows
OD matrix recalculation and simulation settings such as vehicle mix rates to support macroscopic policy analysis

sections.heading: Validation against empirical data
bullets: Predicted traffic demand from the activity-based model is verified against mobile-based metropolitan movement data
Predicted traffic volumes are validated against real traffic counts at VDS stations, achieving a GEH-based accuracy of 90.48%

images.caption: Simulated network flows by time of day: AM peak, non-peak, and PM peak
OD validation against mobile data (ground truth) and GEH-based volume validation at VDS stations

title: Real-time Prediction-based Conflict Measures for Evaluating Pedestrian Crossing Safety
summary: A unified prediction-driven pedestrian-vehicle safety evaluation approach for intelligent intersections, integrating deep learning-based behavior prediction with traffic conflict measures to evaluate pedestrian crossing risk in real time.

paragraphs: The overall objective is a unified prediction-driven pedestrian-vehicle safety evaluation approach for intelligent intersections. Deep learning-based behavior prediction is integrated with traffic conflict measures to enable real-time evaluation of potential and future pedestrian crossing risks across different intersection scenarios, from individual interactions to crowd crossings.

sections.heading: Deterministic conflict evaluation at non-signalized intersections
sections.body: Pedestrian and vehicle time-to-arrival are predicted with deterministic deep learning motion models, formulating the Predicted Lane-based Time-to-Collision (PLTTC) and benchmarking it against TTC, MTTC, and PET. Published in Transportation Research Interdisciplinary Perspectives (2025).

sections.heading: Probabilistic conflict evaluation under behavioral uncertainty
sections.body: Probabilistic trajectories generated with mixture-Gaussian model outputs yield the Stochastic Predicted Time-to-Collision (SPTTC) for early-stage conflict identification with high-variability pedestrian behavior. In revision at IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems.

sections.heading: Real-time crash risk at signalized intersections
sections.body: Deep learning-derived conflict information (Predicted Lane-based Gap Time) is integrated into Extreme Value Theory crash-risk estimation, comparing four EVT formulations against conventional conflict measures. In revision at Analytic Methods in Accident Research.

sections.heading: Crowd crossing risk evaluation
sections.body: Collective pedestrian dynamics are learned with multi-agent trajectory prediction, and a Crowd Gap Time (C-GT) measure evaluates pedestrian-vehicle risk during crowd crossings against baselines such as the Social Force Model. Ongoing.

sections.heading: Behavioral validation with VR and EEG
sections.body: Virtual reality experiments with electroencephalography responses test whether prediction-based conflict measures reflect human-perceived crossing risk and can guide threshold calibration. Ongoing.

images.caption: Multi-camera trajectory data collection at a signalized intersection: camera positions, coverage, and views

title: Human-centric traffic safety
summary: Investigating how drivers, pedestrians, and evacuees make decisions in safety-critical mobility situations through immersive VR experiments, covering micromobility safety, pedestrian-ADAS interaction, and underground/rail environments.

paragraphs: A long-running line of work using immersive VR experiments to measure how drivers, pedestrians, and evacuees actually decide, attend, and move in safety-critical mobility situations. Controlled virtual environments reproduce realistic urban roadways, school zones, tunnels, and subway stations while capturing behavior that would be unsafe or impossible to observe in the field.

sections.heading: Micromobility safety and driver behavior
sections.body: How a driver's own riding experience shapes overtaking maneuvers, lateral distance, and risk-taking around bicycles and e-scooters in mixed traffic, studied with VR-based driving simulators (Transportation Research Part F, 2026).

sections.heading: Pedestrian safety and ADAS
sections.body: How the presentation of safety information — a real-time count of crossing pedestrians, or a UWB-based pedestrian tag — changes braking behavior, cognitive load, and the safety margins drivers leave under uncertainty (Accident Analysis & Prevention, 2024).

sections.heading: Underground and rail safety
sections.body: VR studies of pedestrian evacuation route choice during subway-station fires, in-tunnel information design under varying lighting and speed limits (Tunnelling and Underground Space Technology, 2025), and how age-related perceptual constraints reshape transport disadvantage in railway information systems.

images.caption: Immersive VR experiment platform: Cyberith Virtualizer Elite 2 omnidirectional treadmill with HTC Vive Pro 2 and Unity 3D

title: Analysis Public Transport Accessibility
summary: A comparative framework that measures the gap between planned transit schedules (GTFS) and actual smart-card usage with an extended G2SFCA accessibility model, diagnosing where public transport supply and real travel demand diverge.

paragraphs: Many cities face a spatial mismatch where public transport supply is rigidly misaligned with real-world travel demand, leading to service inefficiency and transportation inequality. This research develops a comparative framework that analyzes the gap between planned transit schedules (GTFS) and actual passenger usage (smart-card OD data) with an extended spatial accessibility model, providing quantitative evidence of where transit resources are wasted or lacking — particularly during off-peak hours.

sections.heading: Extended G2SFCA methodology
sections.body: Diverse services are integrated into a single supply metric using gross floor area, and door-to-door accessibility combines walking and in-vehicle travel times with a Gaussian decay function — moving beyond binary catchment measurements toward a more realistic accessibility index.

sections.heading: Realization rate analysis
sections.body: GTFS supply and smart-card usage are matched at the stop level, and a regression-based realization rate quantifies how far the plan is implemented in reality: alignment is high during peak hours but efficiency drops significantly at midday.

sections.heading: Spatio-temporal mismatch
sections.body: Geographically Weighted Regression reveals a structural midday discrepancy near northern industrial areas of Daejeon — routes rigidly centered on commuting fail to serve midday lifestyle travel such as shopping and medical care — while western and eastern residential clusters use transit efficiently.

sections.heading: Policy implication
sections.body: Transit policy should align the destinations of supply and demand rather than simply increase supply volume, spatially reallocating surplus capacity from low-realization routes to active residential and commercial areas.

images.caption: GWR local coefficients for planned supply vs. realized usage in Daejeon (AM / midday / PM)
Analysis pipeline: data sources, preprocessing, accessibility calculation, and spatial analysis in ArcGIS Pro

title: Goal-Driven Experimental Pipeline for Multi-Vehicle Autonomous Driving in CARLA in ROS2
summary: A CARLA-ROS2 experimental pipeline for goal-driven autonomous driving on Lanelet2 maps, composing multi-vehicle scenarios that mix custom driving policies with rule-based autopilot vehicles to study interaction-aware driving behaviors.

paragraphs: Autonomous driving research increasingly emphasizes interaction among multiple vehicles rather than isolated ego-centric driving, yet controlled experiments mixing heterogeneous vehicle behaviors remain difficult without a unified, goal-driven experimental pipeline.

This research constructs a CARLA-ROS2 pipeline that enables goal-driven autonomous driving using Lanelet2 lane-level maps. Vehicles can be controlled either by custom driving policies or by CARLA's built-in autopilot, supporting flexible composition of multi-vehicle scenarios with varying ratios of autonomous and autopilot-driven vehicles — a foundation for studying interaction-aware driving behaviors, priority decision policies, and future V2X-based coordination.

sections.heading: Core capabilities
bullets: Lanelet2-based goal-driven driving experiments using lane-level map information
Multi-vehicle scenario composition with heterogeneous driving policies
Controlled evaluation of interaction-aware driving behavior under structured map and goal conditions
Modular ROS2 architecture enabling sim-to-real extension
Extensible toward V2X information sharing and priority-based decision policies, including learning-based policies (end-to-end, RL)

images.caption: End-to-end autonomous driving demonstration using CARLA-ROS2 integration, visualized in RViz alongside CARLA
